title: Special Address at the World Economic Forum Virtual Event
titleOriginal: 在世界经济论坛“达沃斯议程”对话会上的特别致辞

paragraphs.text: Professor Schwab, ladies and gentlemen, friends. In this unusual year, a year when the COVID-19 pandemic ravaged the world, it gives me great pleasure to attend this virtual session of the World Economic Forum. At this moment, the pandemic is still raging across the globe. It has affected the lives and health of hundreds of millions, and the global economy faces severe challenges.

Now the question we must answer is: what kind of world do we want to build, and how can we build it? In a world of growing uncertainty, where global challenges multiply and interconnect, the last thing we need is unilateralism, protectionism, or a new Cold War. History has shown us that confrontation, whether in the form of a cold war or a hot war, produces no winners.

Multilateralism is the way forward. The problems facing the world today are global in nature and can only be addressed through global cooperation. No country can solve these problems alone, no matter how powerful. We need to work together, uphold the international system centered on the United Nations, and safeguard the international order underpinned by international law.

We must build an open world economy. Economic globalization has been a powerful engine of world economic growth. It is true that globalization has created new problems, but that is no reason to reject it entirely. Rather, we should work together to steer globalization toward greater openness, inclusiveness, balance, and benefit for all.

In recent years, we have witnessed a troubling rise in protectionism and unilateralism. Some countries have sought to build walls, decouple supply chains, and weaponize trade and technology. This approach runs counter to the laws of economics and the trend of history. Attempting to turn back the clock of globalization or to cut the world into isolated blocs will only harm everyone.

China will continue to pursue a strategy of opening up. We will shorten the negative list for foreign investment, expand the opening of the services sector, and deepen bilateral and multilateral cooperation. The recently signed Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership and the China-EU investment agreement demonstrate our commitment to openness and cooperation.

We need to strengthen macroeconomic policy coordination. The fiscal and monetary policies adopted by major economies have global implications. Major developed countries should adopt responsible economic policies, manage the spillover effects of their policies, and avoid creating additional burdens for developing nations. International economic and financial institutions should play a more active role.

The technological revolution and industrial transformation are advancing at a pace never seen before. Artificial intelligence, big data, cloud computing, and other new technologies are profoundly changing the way we produce, live, and relate to one another. At the same time, the digital divide is widening. We must ensure that the fruits of innovation are shared by all countries and all peoples.

On the matter of technological governance, we should not seek to block others' development or create artificial barriers. Technology is a common achievement of human civilization. Technological blockades and attempts to create technological spheres of influence are doomed to fail. We must work together to establish rules and standards that are fair, just, and non-discriminatory.

Climate change is among the most pressing challenges of our time. China has announced its goals to peak carbon dioxide emissions before 2030 and achieve carbon neutrality before 2060. This was not an easy decision. As the world's largest developing country, it requires enormous effort. But we are committed to this path because it is the right thing to do for our planet.

We must promote a new type of international relations based on mutual respect, equity, justice, and win-win cooperation. The strong should not bully the weak. Decisions should not be made by the few for the many. Differences in social systems and ideologies should not be an excuse for confrontation. Each country has the right to choose its own development path.

China has no intention of fighting either a cold war or a hot war with any country. We do not seek to form exclusive blocs or alliances. We oppose ideological confrontation. What we seek is peaceful coexistence, mutual benefit, and common development. This has been and will continue to be the fundamental principle of China's foreign policy.

The developing world, including China, has contributed enormously to global growth over the past decades. Hundreds of millions of people have been lifted out of poverty. This is an achievement of historic proportions. But the work is not done. We must continue to promote development and bridge the gap between the Global North and the Global South.

The COVID-19 pandemic has laid bare the vulnerabilities in our global governance system. We need to reform and strengthen global governance mechanisms. The United Nations, the World Health Organization, the World Trade Organization, these institutions must be reformed to reflect the new realities of the twenty-first century. But reform means improvement, not demolition.

Vaccine distribution must be equitable. We cannot allow vaccines to become a privilege of the wealthy. China has pledged to make its vaccines a global public good. We are providing vaccines to developing countries and supporting international initiatives like COVAX. This pandemic has taught us that no one is safe until everyone is safe.

The year 2021 marks the beginning of a new chapter. Let us choose cooperation over confrontation, openness over isolation, and mutual benefit over zero-sum competition. Together, we can build a community with a shared future for mankind. Together, we can make the world a better place for current and future generations. Thank you.
